records.ety: [OE. wresten, AS. wr<?/stan; akin to wr<?/<?/ a twisted band, and wrī<?/n to twist. See Writhe.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Wrested; p. pr. & vb. n. Wresting.]

defs: To turn; to twist; esp., to twist or extort by violence; to pull of force away by, or as if by, violent wringing or twisting.
quotes: Our country's cause, That drew our swords, now secret wrests them from our hand.
They instantly wrested the government out of the hands of Hastings.

defs: To turn from truth; to twist from its natural or proper use or meaning by violence; to pervert; to distort.
quotes: Wrest once the law to your authority.
Thou shalt not wrest the judgment of thy poor.
Their arts of wresting, corrupting, and false interpreting the holy text.

defs: To tune with a wrest, or key.
The act of wresting; a wrench; a violent twist; hence, distortion; perversion.
A key to tune a stringed instrument of music.
quotes: The minstrel . . . wore round his neck a silver chain, by which hung the wrest, or key, with which he tuned his harp.

defs: A partition in a water wheel, by which the form of the buckets is determined.
